statement: The state space Σ is a compact topological manifold of finite dimension dim Σ ≥ 1, on which the U(1) action from Axiom 3 acts freely. Smooth (C∞) structure is derived in Theorem 0.0 via the equivariant smoothing theorem, not assumed.
justification: Finite dimensionality gives finitely many degrees of freedom; local Euclidean structure excludes topological pathologies; free orbits ensure every state participates in the cyclic dynamics. Smooth structure then follows from the equivariant smoothing theorem (Illman 1983) applied to the continuous U(1) action.

statement: A Riemannian metric g on Σ that is G_O-invariant: φ_t* g = g for all t.
justification: Now a theorem (Theorem 0.1): any smooth compact manifold admits a Riemannian metric (partition of unity), and Weyl averaging over the compact U(1) action produces a G_O-invariant metric. Replaces the former structural postulate.

statement: The joint state space Σ₁ × Σ₂ carries a symplectic form ω compatible with the U(1) × U(1) action of the constituent observers.
justification: Symplectic structure is the minimal geometric setting for Noether's theorem, which is needed to define relational invariants as conserved quantities.

statement: The coherence measure C is at least C² (twice continuously differentiable), with a positive-definite Hessian metric on state space.
justification: Extends the Riemannian metric of Loop Closure S2 by requiring that coherence curvature itself defines a metric, enabling identification of coherence cost with the action functional.

statement: The coherence geometry (H, g) is a smooth pseudo-Riemannian manifold. The line element ds² = g_μν dxμ dxν is a non-degenerate quadratic form in coordinate differentials.
justification: The individual observer state spaces are Riemannian (positive-definite), but the ambient geometry containing multiple observers must allow for both timelike and spacelike separations. Pseudo-Riemannian rules out Finsler geometry.

statement: The coherence measure C and the metric g are invariant under spacetime translations xμ → xμ + aμ.
justification: Follows from the axioms' location-independence: coherence conservation and observer definitions do not reference absolute position, so the derived geometry inherits translation symmetry.

statement: The metric g_μν is locally determined by the relational invariant density ρ_I, with curvature proportional to ρ_I.
justification: Derived from coherence subadditivity (Axiom 1, C4): the action duality links the spacetime metric to the coherence Hessian; subadditivity ensures the Hessian varies with observer content; action equality transfers this to the spacetime metric. Locality from finite c; coupling to ρ_I from Noether identification.

statement: The self-consistency relation between curvature and coherence content involves at most second derivatives of the metric g_μν.
justification: Restricts the field equation to depend on g, ∂g, and ∂²g only. Combined with d = 4, Lovelock's theorem uniquely determines the Einstein tensor as the geometric side of the equation.

statement: Phase comparison between observers at distinct spacetime points requires smooth parallel transport, described by a connection 1-form A on a principal U(1) bundle P → ℳ over the spacetime manifold.
justification: Relational physics with finite signal speed makes global phase conventions impossible. The minimal structure for comparing phases at separated points is a gauge connection — the gauge-theory analog of the metric in gravity.

statement: The self-consistency condition for the U(1) gauge connection involves at most second derivatives of A_μ — equivalently, at most first derivatives of F_μν.
justification: The gauge-theory analog of second-order locality (Einstein Equations S1). Higher-derivative equations would couple the field to non-local coherence information, violating the principle that the interaction graph determines dynamics locally.

statement: The observer’s phase algebra 𝒶 forms a normed division algebra over ℝ: for all a, b ∈ 𝒶, |ab| = |a| · |b|. Combined with three independent imaginary units (one per spatial axis), this selects 𝒶 = ℍ (quaternions).
justification: Packages three physical requirements: (i) bilinearity of phase composition, (ii) norm-preservation from coherence conservation, (iii) invertibility of phase transformations. With exactly three imaginary units, Hurwitz’s theorem uniquely selects the quaternions.

statement: The self-consistency condition for the SU(2) gauge connection involves at most second derivatives of Wᵃ_μ — equivalently, at most first derivatives of the field strength Wᵃ_μν.
justification: Identical in motivation to Electromagnetism S2 and Einstein Equations S1: locality restricts the field equations to depend on the connection and its first derivatives only.

shortName: Algebraic saturation at each bootstrap level
statement: Each level of the bootstrap hierarchy saturates the phase algebra to the next normed division algebra via the Cayley-Dickson construction: ℝ → ℂ → ℍ → 𝕆. Hurwitz’s theorem terminates the sequence at 𝕆.
justification: Extends the algebraic completeness postulate (Weak Interaction S1) to the full bootstrap hierarchy. Now derived as a theorem: Bootstrap → Division Algebras proves that coherence conservation forces Cayley-Dickson doubling at each level.

statement: The coherence measure restricted to transition amplitudes satisfies C(|ψ⟩) = ⟨ψ|ψ⟩ = Σ_k |ψ_k|².
justification: Now a theorem (Theorem 0.1): the unique continuous, U(1)-invariant, composition-compatible coherence functional on quantum states is the squared norm, via the Cauchy multiplicative equation. Derived in Coherence as Physical Primitive (Theorem 4.1).

statement: Every Type III interaction generates a relational invariant whose operator representation is the conserved quantity associated with the interaction Hamiltonian via Noether's theorem.
justification: Connects the framework's interaction types to quantum measurement: the measurement basis is determined by the conserved quantities of the observer–system interaction, not by an external choice.

statement: Transition amplitudes are single-valued on the universal cover of configuration space. The wave function is a section of a flat line bundle with holonomy Hom(π₁(Q), U(1)).
justification: The standard Laidlaw–DeWitt quantization condition: amplitudes must be consistent under particle exchange, which constrains the wave function to be either symmetric or antisymmetric in d = 3.

statement: The formation of stable composite observers at each bootstrap level is mediated by coherence tunneling with WKB probability P ~ exp(−V·a/ħ).
justification: The bootstrap hierarchy produces new composite observers at each level. Tunneling through coherence barriers provides the mechanism, and the exponential WKB suppression naturally generates the large mass ratios observed in the particle spectrum.

statement: Each fermion generation corresponds to a class of half-integer winding loops classified by dominant alignment with one of three independent generators of so(3), partitioned by Voronoi decomposition of S².
justification: Since d = 3 spatial dimensions provide exactly three independent rotation axes, the half-integer winding loops of fermions naturally partition into three sectors, one per generation.

statement: In the confining phase, the vacuum state minimizes the total coherence cost subject to the constraint that all asymptotic states are color singlets.
justification: Extends the bootstrap self-consistency principle to the non-perturbative regime: the vacuum should be the state of minimal coherence cost, generalizing the perturbative requirement to the confining phase.

statement: The coherence geometry has a minimum resolvable scale ℓ_P = √(ħG/c³) (the Planck length). Below ℓ_P, the coherence cost of loop closure exceeds available coherence for any observer.
justification: Combines gravity (G), quantum mechanics (ħ), and the speed of light (c) into a fundamental resolution limit. No observer loop can exist at sub-Planck scales, providing the UV cutoff that leads to holographic entropy bounds.
